narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would fail it. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and test scores, and schools use it to ensure they admit those most likely to succeed. For you, as a Black male with an upper-middle-class family background, the model weighed your third-year school decile ranking as the strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction—your position in the third decile there, lower than the typical student's fifth-decile average, signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. Next, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 stood out positively, above the average of 3.21, and it notably pulled the prediction back toward passing by showing solid prior academic strength. Your LSAT score of 33, however, which is below the dataset average of 36.7, contributed toward the failure side, reinforcing concerns about exam readiness. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority of applicants, so it didn't sway things much either way. Compared to typical students who pass, your third-year grades were a bigger red flag, while your undergrad record was a strength that nearly balanced it out. These top three factors—your later school performance, undergrad GPA, and LSAT—dominated the model's thinking for your profile. It's tough to hear, especially given the barriers many Black students like yourself face in competitive academic tracks, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's a data-driven risk assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your third-year record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with an updated profile that highlights any recent achievements. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these areas, and admissions teams value persistence and growth. You have the foundation—building on it could shift the odds in your favor.
